label: Squarish 2BR / 2 baths CLUSTERED, FRONT-BACK split — the entire front (street-facing) half is public: living room on the left, kitchen on the right, open-plan, no separate dining room. The entire rear half is private, arranged as ONE row: master, ensuite, common bath, and standard bedroom side by side (in that order), each dooring directly across the public/private boundary wall except ensuite (master's exclusive, no public door) and common (which shares its wet wall with ensuite instead). No hallway.

adjacencies.note: ensuite is master's exclusive — door on master's EAST wall (ensuite now sits beside master in the rear row, not behind it). Ensuite and common cluster together in the middle of the row, between master and standard.

ensuite and common share a plumbing wall — the two-bath cluster in the middle of the rear row, between master and standard. Replaces the old common<->kitchen wet-stack (no longer geometrically adjacent now that common has moved into the row's middle).

common bath's east wall = standard's west wall — shared wall only, no door (bedroom<->common-bath is always wall-only per the codebase's generic rule). Acoustic buffer between the bath cluster and standard.
master's south wall = living's north wall at the front/rear boundary — a real door, no hall. Master reaches the rest of the house only through this one door.
standard's south wall overlaps living's north wall — direct door. 1.2 m floor gives 0.8 m clear door + 0.15 m frame each side.

common bath's only door — south wall opens into living. With ensuite (wet_core, no door) on one side and standard (wall-only) on the other, this is common's sole access, same idiom as the squarish side_split_bath_gr family: both bedrooms reach the bath by stepping out through their own door into the public band and back in.
living + kitchen form one open public band — no separate dining room in this variant. The entire front half of the house is one continuous open space.

lot_adjustment_profiles.name: compact_sq_front_back_split_baths_ds_lk
lot_adjustment_profiles.rationale: Splitting the lot front-back halves the usable depth per band compared to the vertical-split topologies, so compact lots get tight fast — the whole private band has to fit master, ensuite, common, and standard side by side in one row of depth. auto_apply floors kitchen width and both bath areas so neither collapses to its hard minimum by default; master is capped at 18 sqm so its priority weight doesn't crowd the bath cluster or standard sharing its row. preferred_apply bumps ensuite and kitchen further where the envelope allows.

setback_elements.note: placed on the LIVING side (left), not the kitchen side — deviates from the catalog default of pairing carport with kitchen's side, because the dirty kitchen already claims the kitchen-side setback here (kitchen is at the front, not the rear, so it can't push its service tail into a rear setback like every other topology does).

moved from rear_setback to side_setback (kitchen-side/right) — a direct consequence of the front-back flip. Kitchen sits in the front band near the street in this topology, so it has no rear wall to extend a service tail from; the dirty kitchen instead extends off kitchen's outer side wall.
grouped with dirty_kitchen on the kitchen side for the same reason.
on the living side, opposite the kitchen/service side.

notes: Squarish catalog topology — 2BR / 2 baths CLUSTERED, FRONT-BACK split. Every other topology in the catalog splits the lot vertically (bedrooms on one side, LDK on the other, both running front-to-rear). This one splits it horizontally instead: the entire front half of the house is public, the entire rear half is private, and one wall spanning the full width separates them.
Rear (private, one row): [master | ensuite | common | standard]

Revision history: originally had ensuite stacked BEHIND master (its own sub-column) and common stacked behind kitchen sharing a wet-stack with it. Both of those arrangements were geometrically infeasible — master was declared rear_anchored while also having ensuite stacked behind it (contradiction: master can't touch the rear wall AND have another room behind it), and kitchen is unconditionally hardcoded in the solver to always touch the rear wall, directly contradicting having common stacked behind it. Reworked so ensuite and common cluster together in the MIDDLE of a single rear row, between master and standard — nothing in the row has another private room behind it, so rear_anchored is safe for all four, and common no longer needs to touch kitchen at all.

No hallway. Master and standard each door directly across the front/rear boundary wall into living. Ensuite is master's exclusive (wet_core with common, door only from master). Common's only door faces living — same circulation idiom as 1s_2br_sq_side_split_bath_gr: both bedrooms reach the bath by stepping into the shared public room and back in.
Wet stack: ensuite and common share a plumbing wall (wet_core) in the middle of the row — a compact two-bath cluster, no longer tied to kitchen's plumbing.

IMPORTANT deviation from the rest of the catalog: because kitchen sits at the FRONT of the house (street side) rather than the rear, the dirty kitchen and service area can't extend into a rear_setback the way they do in every vertical-split topology — there's no rear wall behind kitchen to extend from. They're relocated to the side_setback on kitchen's side instead. Carport is pushed to the opposite side (living's side) to avoid crowding that same setback strip.

KNOWN OPEN RISK (not yet resolved as of this revision): kitchen's hardcoded rear-wall anchor (solver.py, unconditional for every topology with a kitchen room) may still conflict with kitchen needing to sit in the FRONT band next to living — this is a structural tension in the front-back-split concept itself, not something fixable by rearranging the bedroom/bath row alone. Verify solver feasibility empirically before relying on this topology; if still infeasible, the remaining fix would need to either loosen the kitchen-rear hardcoded rule for front-band kitchens, or accept that living+kitchen end up spanning the full depth in their own width-column (no longer a clean single dividing wall).

Trade-offs vs. the vertical-split topologies: privacy is stronger (one wall separates ALL sleeping/bathing space from anything a visitor sees), but the whole private band depends on one row of depth — all four private rooms have to fit side by side within half the lot's depth, so this wants a genuinely squarish (not shallow) lot.
When brief.carport_side='right', the runner mirrors the topology: living/carport move right, kitchen/service move left.
Fallback: 1s_2br_sq_side_split_bath_gr — drops to the standard vertical-split single-bath topology on lots too shallow to support two full-width bands.
